Author: Aibar, Alberto; Bois, Julien E.; Generelo, Eduardo; Bengoechea, Enrique Garcia; Paillard, Thierry; Zaragoza, Javier
Title: Effect of weather, school transport, and perceived neighborhood characteristics on moderate to vigorous physical activity levels of adolescents from two european cities
Study: Evaluation and Promotion of Physical Activity
Finding: There were no significant associations between perceived walkability and MVPA

Author: Aznar, Susana; Jimenez-Zazo, Fabio; Romero-Blanco, Cristina; Gomez, Santiago F.; Homs, Clara; Warnberg, Julia; Medrano, Maria; Gusi, Narcis; Gonzalez-Gross, Marcela; Marin-Cascales, Elena; Gonzalez-Valeiro, Miguel angel; Serra-Majem, Lluis; Terrados, Nicolas; Tur, Josep A.; Segu, Marta; Lassale, Camille; Colom-Fernandez, Antoni; Labayen, Idoia; Sanchez-Gomez, Jesus; Alcaraz, Pedro Emilio; Sevilla-Sanchez, Marta; Zapico, Augusto G.; Herrera-Ramos, Estefania; Pulgar, Susana; Bibilonii, Maria del Mar; Sistac, Clara; Schroder, Helmut; Molina-Garcia, Javier
Title: Walkability and socio-economic status in relation to walking, playing and sports practice in a representative Spanish sample of youth: The PASOS study
PA Type: MVPA; organized sports; other PA; walking
Other PA: Total PA (self report); sports participation (self report)
Finding: Walkability was associated with walking behavior, not with any other PA term including MVPA. SES was associated with sports participation and MVPA

Author: Bird, Madeleine; Datta, Geetanjali D.; Chinerman, Deanna; Kakinami, Lisa; Mathieu, Marie-Eve; Henderson, Melanie; Barnett, Tracie A.
Title: Associations of neighborhood walkability with moderate to vigorous physical activity: an application of compositional data analysis comparing compositional and non-compositional approaches
Publication Title: INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF BEHAVIORAL NUTRITION AND PHYSICAL ACTIVITY
Study: Quebec Adipose and Lifestyle Investigation in Youth
Finding: MVPA was significantly associated with walkability in girls only, not boys

Author: Buck, Christoph; Eiben, Gabriele; Lauria, Fabio; Konstabel, Kenn; Page, Angie; Ahrens, Wolfgang; Pigeot, Iris; on behalf of the IDEFICS and the I.Family consortia
Title: Urban Moveability and physical activity in children: longitudinal results from the IDEFICS and I.Family cohort
Publication Title: International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity
Finding: Moveability index positively affected MVPA in girls, lessened decline in LPA for boys. Public open spaces seemed more important in childhood, while residential density and intersection density more important in adolescence.

Author: Molina-García, Javier; Campos, Sergio; García-Massó, Xavier; Herrador-Colmenero, Manuel; Gálvez-Fernández, Patricia; Molina-Soberanes, Daniel; Queralt, Ana; Chillón, Palma
Title: Different neighborhood walkability indexes for active commuting to school are necessary for urban and rural children and adolescents
Publication Title: International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity
Finding: Street connectivity and residential density were positively related to ATS in urban children, but negatively related in rural children.

Author: Buck, Christoph; Tkaczick, Tobias; Pitsiladis, Yannis; De Bourdehaudhuij, Ilse; Reisch, Lucia; Ahrens, Wolfgang; Pigeot, Iris
Title: Objective Measures of the Built Environment and Physical Activity in Children: From Walkability to Moveability
Measure(s) of Walkability: Moveability; Frank excluding retail; Frank including retail
Finding: Moveability index was significantly associated with MVPA in school children, not walkability. No significant associations for pre-school children.

Author: Bucko, Agnes G.; Porter, Dwayne E.; Saunders, Ruth; Shirley, Lynn; Dowda, Marsha; Pate, Russell R.
Title: Walkability indices and children's walking behavior in rural vs. urban areas
Measure(s) of Walkability: Walk Score; Frank excluding retail; NWI
Other transport: Walking for exercise (self report); Walking for transport (self report)
Measure of PA: Walking for exercise (self report); Walking for transportation (self report); Total PA (accelerometer)
Finding: NWI significantly related to reduced walking for transportation overall. Frank significantly related to reduced walking for exercise in rural children. Walk score and NWI related to reduced walking for transportation in urban children. Frank and Walk Score related to reduced Total PA in urban children.

Author: Rahman, Mohammad Lutfur; Pocock, Tessa; Moore, Antoni; Mandic, Sandra
Title: Active Transport to School and School Neighbourhood Built Environment across Urbanisation Settings in Otago, New Zealand
Publication Title: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
Finding: ATS rates negatively correlated with school-area walkability. Perception of traffic safety negatively correlated with ATS rates.

Author: Caetano, Isabella Toledo; Miranda, Valter Paulo Neves; Santos, Fernanda Karina dos; Amorim, Paulo Roberto dos Santos
Title: Ecological correlates related to adolescent movement behaviors: A latent class analysis
Finding: Having better street connectivity, diverse land use, traffic safety, walking easy, made participants more likely to be in the active and non-sedentary class than active and sedentary.

Author: Caetano, Isabella Toledo; Fermino, Rogerio Cesar; Falcao, Renato de Oliveira; Amorim, Paulo Roberto dos Santos
Title: Built environment and physical activity in adolescents: Use of the kernel density estimation and the walkability index
Finding: Highest quartile of walkability was associated with higher odds of sufficient PA

Author: Macdonald, Laura; McCrorie, Paul; Nicholls, Natalie; Olsen, Jonathan R.
Title: Active commute to school: does distance from school or walkability of the home neighbourhood matter? A national cross-sectional study of children aged 10-11 years, Scotland, UK
Finding: Odds of active transport were less likely for those in the least walkable areas only.

Author: Cain, Kelli L.; Gavand, Kavita A.; Conway, Terry L.; Geremia, Carrie M.; Millstein, Rachel A.; Frank, Lawrence D.; Saelens, Brian E.; Adams, Marc A.; Glanz, Karen; King, Abby C.; Sallis, James F.
Title: Developing and validating an abbreviated version of the microscale audit for pedestrian streetscapes (MAPS-abbreviated)
Study: Neighborhood impact on kids; teen environment and neighborhood
Other PA: Leisure PA in neighborhood (self report); leisure PA in neighborhood (parent report)
Other transport: Walk for transport (parent reported); Walk for transport (self reported)
Finding: Many different findings. Interesting to me is the cul-de-sacs were significantly related to PA in neighborhood for children, but not with objective MVPA

Author: Cain, Kelli L.; Millstein, Rachel A.; Sallis, James F.; Conway, Terry L.; Gavand, Kavita A.; Frank, Lawrence D.; Saelens, Brian E.; Geremia, Carrie M.; Chapman, James; Adams, Marc A.; Glanz, Karen; King, Abby C.
Title: Contribution of streetscape audits to explanation of physical activity in four age groups based on the Microscale Audit of Pedestrian Streetscapes (MAPS)
Study: Neighborhood impact on kids; teen environment and neighborhood
Other PA: Leisure and neighborhood PA (self report or parent report)
Finding: MAPS scores were related to MVPA for children and older adults. Active transport related to MAPS scores across the board.

Author: Tewahade, Selam; Li, Kaigang; Goldstein, Risë B.; Haynie, Denise; Iannotti, Ronald J.; Simons-Morton, Bruce
Title: Association between the Built Environment and Active Transportation among U.S. Adolescents
Finding: Walkability associated with active travel

Author: Campbell, JE.; Janssen, I.
Title: The neighborhood physical environment and the 24-hour movement behavior composition among children
Publication Title: International journal of environmental health research
Finding: No significant relationship between walkability and movement behaviors

Author: Lee, Sungmin; Lee, Chanam; Nam, Ji Won; Abbey-Lambertz, Mark; Mendoza, Jason
Title: School Walkability Index: Application of Environmental Audit Tool and GIS
Measure(s) of Walkability: audit-based school WI; GIS-based school WI
Finding: Audit-based WI was associated with ATS and before-school MVPA for children living within 1.5 km of school. GIS-based WI associated with before-school MVPA, not ATS
Other Notes: Before-school MVPA was used to estimate MVPA obtained on the journey to school. This is dependent on children living close to school, which children are less likely to live close to school when the area is less walkable

Author: Carlson, Jordan A.; Saelens, Brian E.; Kerr, Jacqueline; Schipperijn, Jasper; Conway, Terry L.; Frank, Lawrence D.; Chapman, Jim E.; Glanz, Karen; Cain, Kelli L.; Sallis, James F.
Title: Association between neighborhood walkability and GPS-measured walking, bicycling and vehicle time in adolescents
Finding: Participants in highly walkable neighborhoods engaged in more walking and bicycling
Reason: No association between walkability and PA

Author: Deforche, B.; Van Dyck, D.; Verloigne, M.; De Bourdeaudhuij, I.
Title: Perceived social and physical environmental correlates of physical activity in older adolescents and the moderating effect of self-efficacy
Finding: Land-use mix, street connectivity, and aesthetics positively related to active transport. Access to recreational facilities negatively related to active transport and leisure-time sports. Perceived safety from traffic positively related to leisure time sports.
Reason: Only PA outcome is leisure-time sports

Author: Chalikavada, Radhika; Broder, Jonathan C.; O’Hara, Rebecca L.; Xue, Wenyue; Gasevic, Danijela
Title: The association between neighbourhood walkability and after-school physical activity in Australian schoolchildren
Finding: Living in a walker's paradise was associated with more afterschool PA.
Reason: Does not include MVPA or total PA

Author: Cheah, WL.; Chang, CT.; Saimon, R.
Title: Environment factors associated with adolescents' body mass index, physical activity and physical fitness in Kuching South City, Sarawak: a cross-sectional study
Publication Title: International journal of adolescent medicine and health
Other PA: PA before school (self report); PA during school (self report); PA after school (self report); Total PA (self report)
Finding: No significant relationship between walkability and PA

Author: Colley, Rachel C; Christidis, Tanya; Mechaud, Isabelle; Tjepkema, Michael; Ross, Nancy A.
Title: The association between walkable neighbourhoods and physical activity across the lifespan
Study: Canadian Health Measures Survey and Canadian Community Health Survey
PA Type: LPA; MVPA; organized PA; other PA
Other PA: LPA (accelerometer); Total PA (self report); Total PA (parent report)
Sample Size: 4030 for 3-11. Sample size for 12-17 not reported.
Finding: Walkability was only significantly associated with accelerometer-based MVPA and accelerometer-based LPA for the 12-17 age range, not for 3-11. Walkability was not significantly associated with step counts. Walkability was significantly associated with self-reported transportation PA for 12-17 (not measured for <12). Only adults had a linear trend upward in MVPA with increasing walkability class. There was a downward trend for parent-reported PA for ages 5-11 with increasing walkability class.
Other Notes: Can-ALE is explicitly described as a walkability index

Author: Carlson, Jordan A.; Sallis, James F.; Kerr, Jacqueline; Conway, Terry L.; Cain, Kelli; Frank, Lawrence D.; Saelens, Brian E.
Title: Built environment characteristics and parent active transportation are associated with active travel to school in youth age 12-15
Finding: Perceived street connectivity, perceived pedestrian safety, objective residential density all psoitively associated with ATS
Other Notes: Excludes children who live more than 2 miles from their school

Author: D'Haese, Sara; Van Dyck, Delfien; De Bourdeaudhuij, Ilse; Deforche, Benedicte; Cardon, Greet
Title: The association between the parental perception of the physical neighborhood environment and children's location-specific physical activity
Finding: Children more likely to be active in nearby streets and on sidewalks if their parents pareceived lower street connectivity, higher land use mix, and more crime safety. No relationship with MVPA

Author: D’Haese, Sara; Van Dyck, Delfien; De Bourdeaudhuij, Ilse; Deforche, Benedicte; Cardon, Greet
Title: The association between objective walkability, neighborhood socio-economic status, and physical activity in Belgian children
Publication Title: International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity
Finding: In low SES neighborhoods, walkability was positively related to walking for transportation during leisure time and was negatively related to sports during leisure time. In high SES neighborhoods, walkability was unrelated to children's PA. No relationship between walkability and ATS or MVPA were found.

Author: Datar, Ashlesha; Nicosia, Nancy; Wong, Elizabeth; Shier, Victoria
Title: Neighborhood environment and children's physical activity and body mass index: Evidence from military personnel installation assignments
Study: Military Teenagers Environments, Exercise, and Nutrition Study
Other PA: self report VPA; self report MPA
Finding: Street connectivity had negative association with VPA. Crime safety was associated with more MPA

Author: Giles-Corti, Billie; Wood, Gina; Pikora, Terri; Learnihan, Vincent; Bulsara, Max; Van Niel, Kimberly; Timperio, Anna; McCormack, Gavin; Villanueva, Karen
Title: School site and the potential to walk to school: the impact of street connectivity and traffic exposure in school neighborhoods
Finding: Children in schools with high connectivity and high traffic exposure were less likely to walk, while high connectivity and low traffic exposure more likely to walk.

Author: De Meester, Femke; Van Dyck, Delfien; De Bourdeaudhuij, Ilse; Cardon, Greet
Title: Parental perceived neighborhood attributes: associations with active transport and physical activity among 10-12 year old children and the mediating role of independent mobility
Other transport: step count (pedometer and self report); step count (accelerometer)
Measure of PA: Total PA (parent report); step count (pedometer and self report); step count (accelerometer)
Finding: Many of the subscales in the NEWS-Y were positively associated with total PA and step counts
Other Notes: This is a type of total PA that is just the sum of other categories of PA

Author: Lavergne, Valerie; Butler, Gregory; Prince, Stephanie A.; Contreras, Gisele
Title: Associations between school-level environment and individual-level factors of walking and cycling to school in Canadian youth
Finding: Walkability positively associated with walking to school, not associated with biking to school.

Author: De Meester, Femke; Van Dyck, Delfien; De Bourdeaudhuij, Ilse; Deforche, Benedicte; Cardon, Greet
Title: Do psychosocial factors moderate the association between neighborhood walkability and adolescents' physical activity?
Finding: Walkability was positively associated with PA  for adolescents in low-income neighborhoods who perceived many barriers and few benefits, while for adolescents who perceved few barriers and many benefits, the PA level was high, irrespective of neighborhood walkability. For adolescents in high income neighborhoos, no psychosocial attributes moderated the association between neighbrohood walkability and PA

Author: Nelson, Norah M.; Woods, Catherine B.
Title: Neighborhood perceptions and active commuting to school among adolescent boys and girls
Finding: Gendered differences in perception of built environment. Gendered differences in effect. Some subscales supported ATS, some prohibited

Author: De Meester, Femke; Van Dyck, Delfien; De Bourdeaudhuij, Ilse; Deforche, Benedicte; Sallis, James F.; Cardon, Greet
Title: Active living neighborhoods: is neighborhood walkability a key element for Belgian adolescents?
Finding: Only in low-SES neighborhoods was walkability associated with MVPA. ATS had no association with walkability

Author: Dias, Arieli Fernandes; Gaya, Anelise Reis; Santos, Maria Paula; Brand, Caroline; Pizarro, Andreia Nogueira; Fochesatto, Camila Felin; Mendes, Thiago Monteiro; Mota, Jorge; Araujo Gaya, Adroaldo Cezar
Title: Neighborhood environmental factors associated leisure walking in adolescents
Finding: Walkability associated with leisure walking only in middle SES girls, not associated in boys

Author: Doan, N.; Srugo, SA.; Prince, SA.; Colley, RC.; Rainham, DG.; Manyanga, T.; Butler, GP.; Larouche, R.; Turner, SE.; Lang, JJ.
Title: Independent and joint associations of neighbourhood greenness and walkability with transportational and recreational physical activity among youth and adults in Canada
Finding: Walkability x vegetation was significantly positively related to active transport in youth. Walkability had no other significant associations for youth.
Reason: Does not include MVPA or total PA

Author: Duncan, Susan C.; Strycker, Lisa A.; Chaumeton, Nigel R.; Cromley, Ellen K.
Title: Relations of neighborhood environment influences, physical activity, and active transportation to/from school across african american, latino american, and white girls in the united states
Finding: Active transport was significantly related to walkability, not MVPA

Author: Durand, Casey P.; Dunton, Genevieve F.; Spruijt-Metz, Donna; Pentz, Mary Ann
Title: Does community type moderate the relationship between parent perceptions of the neighborhood and physical activity in children?
Finding: Lack of cul-de-sacs and walking infrastructure was positively related to MVPA> Social interaction was negatively related to MVPA. No other NEWS factors had a relationship with MVPA. For ATS, diverse land use and hard parking were negatively associated. Social interaction was positively associated.

Author: Gerards, SMPL.; Van, DHH., Kann; Kremers, SPJ.; Jansen, MWJ.; Gubbels, JS.
Title: Do parenting practices moderate the association between the physical neighbourhood environment and changes in children's time spent at various physical activity levels? An exploratory longitudinal study
Finding: Walkability was significantly associated with a reduction in sedentary behavior at 18 month follow up

Author: Cottagiri, Susanna Abraham; De Groh, Margaret; Srugo, Sebastian A.; Jiang, Ying; Hamilton, Hayley A.; Ross, Nancy A.; Villeneuve, Paul J.
Title: Are school-based measures of walkability and greenness associated with modes of commuting to school? Findings from a student survey in Ontario, Canada
Publication Title: CANADIAN JOURNAL OF PUBLIC HEALTH-REVUE CANADIENNE DE SANTE PUBLIQUE
Study: Ontario Student Drug Use and Health Survey
Finding: School area having higher walkability was associated with higher odds of active transport to school

Author: Graziose, Matthew M.; Gray, Heewon Lee; Quinn, James; Rundle, Andrew G.; Contento, Isobel R.; Koch, Pamela A.
Title: Association between the built environment in school neighborhoods with physical activity among new york city children, 2012
Finding: No significant relationship between walkability and PA

Author: Hinckson, Erica; Cerin, Ester; Mavoa, Suzanne; Smith, Melody; Badland, Hannah; Stewart, Tom; Duncan, Scott; Schofield, Grant
Title: Associations of the perceived and objective neighborhood environment with physical activity and sedentary time in New Zealand adolescents
Publication Title: The International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity
Finding: Both objective and subjective indices showed positive relationship with MVPA

Author: Napier, Melissa A.; Brown, Barbara B.; Werner, Carol M.; Gallimore, Jonathan
Title: Walking to school: Community design and child and parent barriers
Finding: Children in the walkable or mixed walkable communities were much more likely to walk to school vs the traditional community

Author: Orellana, Daniel; Hermida, Carla; Quezada, Adriana; Andrade, Jorge; Ballari, Daniela
Title: Children's active mobility to school: Evidence from two andean cities
Finding: eMAPS was positively associated with active travel to school

Author: De Meester, Femke; Van Dyck, Delfien; De Bourdeaudhuij, Ilse; Deforche, Benedicte; Cardon, Greet
Title: Does the perception of neighborhood built environmental attributes influence active transport in adolescents?
Publication Title: INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF BEHAVIORAL NUTRITION AND PHYSICAL ACTIVITY
Finding: Connectivity had strong positive relationship with walking land use mix diversity and walking infrastructure had anegative association. Traditional walkability measures were not significant for walking for transport during leisure time.

Author: Hobin, Erin; Leatherdale, Scott; Manske, Steve; Dubin, Joel; Elliott, Susan; Veugelers, Paul
Title: A multilevel examination of factors of the school environment and time spent in moderate to vigorous physical activity among a sample of secondary school students in grades 9-12 in Ontario, Canada
Finding: Walkability index was negatively associated with time spent in PA

Author: Wang, Yinuo; Liu, Yang; Song, Shenzhi; Gittelsohn, Joel; Ouellette, Miranda; Ma, Yanan; Wen, Deliang
Title: Individual, parental and built environmental features as influencing factors of active travel to school in northeast China: findings from a cross-sectional study
Finding: Land use mix was positively associated with ATS, but not intersection density or redicential density. Suggestion from authors that NEWS-Y may not be appropriate for Chinese children

Author: Hunter, Stephen; Battista, Kate; Leatherdale, Scott T.; Spence, John C.; Carson, Valerie
Title: Longitudinal associations between the school built environment and adolescents? physical activity: Evidence from the COMPASS study.
Finding: Walk Score was not significantly related to MVPA at a single time point, but it was associated with a smaller decline of MVPA over time

Author: Jacobs, Jane; Crooks, Nic; Allender, Steven; Strugnell, Claudia; Backholer, Kathryn; Nichols, Melanie
Title: Is the physical activity environment surrounding primary schools associated with students' weight status, physical activity or active transport, in regional areas of Victoria, Australia? A cross-sectional study
Finding: Walkability asssociated with active transport, but not with MVPA

Author: Janssen, I.; King, N.
Title: Walkable school neighborhoods are not playable neighborhoods
Study: Canadian Health Behaviour in School-Aged Children Survey
Finding: Children in lower walkable neighborhoods got more physical activity than in the most walkable neighborhoods. They were less likely to have at least 60 minutes of daily MVPA
Other Notes: not really MVPA, just total PA

Author: Kligerman, Morton; Sallis, James F.; Ryan, Sherry; Frank, Lawrence D.; Nader, Philip R.
Title: Association of neighborhood design and recreation environment variables with physical activity and body mass index in adolescents
Publication Title: American journal of health promotion: AJHP
Finding: MVPA was significantly positively associated with walkability index

Author: Larouche, R.; Blanchette, S.; Faulkner, G.; Riazi, N.; Trudeau, F.; Tremblay, MS.
Title: Correlates of children's physical activity: a canadian multisite study
Publication Title: Medicine and science in sports and exercise
Finding: No significant association between walkability and MVPA

Author: Laxer, Rachel E.; Janssen, Ian
Title: The proportion of youths' physical inactivity attributable to neighbourhood built environment features
Study: Health Behaviour in School-Aged Children Survey (HBSC)
Finding: Walkability associated with increased inactivity. Cul-de-sac density associated with less inactivity.

Author: Rothman, Linda; Hagel, Brent; Howard, Andrew; Cloutier, Marie Soleil; Macpherson, Alison; Aguirre, Alberto Nettel; McCormack, Gavin R.; Fuselli, Pamela; Buliung, Ron; HubkaRao, Tate; Ling, Rebecca; Zanotto, Moreno; Rancourt, Mathieu; Winters, Meghan
Title: Active school transportation and the built environment across Canadian cities: Findings from the child active transportation safety and the environment (CHASE) study
Study: Child Active Transportation Safety and the Environment (CHASE)
Finding: Higher Walk Score was associated with higher odds of AST

Author: de Bont, Jeroen; Marquez, Sandra; Fernandez-Barres, Silvia; Warembourg, Charline; Koch, Sarah; Persavento, Cecilia; Fochs, Silvia; Peya, Nuria; de Castro, Montserrat; Fossati, Serena; Nieuwenhuijsen, Mark; Basagana, Xavier; Casas, Maribel; Duarte-Salles, Talita; Vrijheid, Martine
Title: Urban environment and obesity and weight-related behaviours in primary school children
Other PA: Total PA (self report); vigorous PA (self report)
Finding: No significant differences in PA between urban clusters
Reason: Cluster analysis. Not a direct association.

Author: Wang, Xiaobo; Conway, Terry L.; Cain, Kelli L.; Frank, Lawrence D.; Saelens, Brian E.; Geremia, Carrie; Kerr, Jacqueline; Glanz, Karen; Carlson, Jordan A.; Sallis, James F.
Title: Interactions of psychosocial factors with built environments in explaining adolescents' active transportation
Other transport: Active transport index (self report); Non-school active transport index (self report)
Finding: Both walkability and MAPS associated with increased active transportation. There were also significant interaction effects between self-efficacy and the walkability index, as well as barrier to activity in neighborhood and MAPS, and gender, MAPS active travel, and athletic ability

Author: Vanwolleghem, Griet; Schipperijn, Jasper; Gheysen, Freja; Cardon, Greet; De Bourdeaudhuij, Ilse; Van Dyck, Delfien
Title: Children's GPS-determined versus self-reported transport in leisure time and associations with parental perceptions of the neighborhood environment
Finding: Significant associations between parentally perceived walkability and cycling, parentally perceived walking facilities with weekend walking trips. No other significant associations were found.

Author: Van Dyck, Delfien; De Meester, Femke; Cardon, Greet; Deforche, Benedicte; De Bourdeaudhuij, Ilse
Title: Physical environmental attributes and active transportation in belgium: What about adults and adolescents living in the same neighborhoods?
Finding: Perceived walkability was only associated with active transport for adults, not adolescents

Author: Lee, Jihyun; Healy, Sean; Haegele, Justin A.
Title: Environmental and social determinants of leisure-time physical activity in children with autism spectrum disorder
Other PA: leisure-time PA (parent report); days exercised at least 60 minutes (parent report)
Finding: No association between Walk Score and PA

Author: McDonald, Kelsey; Hearst, Mary; Farbakhsh, Kian; Patnode, Carrie; Forsyth, Ann; Sirard, John; Lytle, Leslie
Title: Adolescent physical activity and the built environment: A latent class analysis approach
Study: Transdisciplinary Research on Energetics and Cancer - Identifying Determinants of Eating and Activity
Measure(s) of Walkability: Frank excluding land use mix; stratified walkability
Finding: No significant differences in PA between walkability classes

Author: Lee, Rebecca E.; Soltero, Erica G.; Jáuregui, Alejandra; Mama, Scherezade K.; Barquera, Simón; Jauregui, Edtna; Lopez y Taylor, Juan; Ortiz-Hernández, Luis; Lévesque, Lucie
Title: Disentangling Associations of Neighborhood Street Scale Elements With Physical Activity in Mexican School Children
Measure(s) of Walkability: Pedestrian Environment Data Scan; Frank excluding retail
Other PA: Outdoor play (parent report); Formal PA (parent report)
Measure of PA: Outdoor play (parent report); Formal PA (parent report)
Finding: Walkability lowers odds of outdoor play; Walkability lowers odds of formal PA/
Reason: Does not include MVPA or total PA

Author: Leung, Gregorich, CW; Yen, IH
Title: Measuring the neighborhood environment: associations with young girls' energy intake and expenditure in a cross-sectional study
Publication Title: International journal of behavioral nutrition and physical activity
Study: Cohort Study of Young Girls' Nutrituion, Environment, and Transitions
Finding: Walkability was only significantly associated with PA for Hispanic girls
Other Notes: Walkability was not associated with neighborhood deprivation

Author: Van Dyck, Delfien; Cardon, Greet; Deforche, Benedicte; De Bourdeaudhuij, Ilse
Title: Lower neighbourhood walkability and longer distance to school are related to physical activity in Belgian adolescents
Other transport: Step count (pedometer and self report); active travel (self report)
Finding: Living in the suburban area was associated with more cycling for transport and to school. No other significant relationships were found.

Author: Noonan, RJ.; Boddy, LM.; Knowles, ZR.; Fairclough, SJ.
Title: Fitness, fatness and active school commuting among liverpool schoolchildren
Publication Title: International journal of environmental research and public health
Finding: Active commuting was positively related to land-use mix and street connectivity. It was negatively related to neighborhood aesthetics.

Author: Loh, Venurs H. Y.; Veitch, Jenny; Salmon, Jo; Cerin, Ester; Thornton, Lukar; Mavoa, Suzanne; Villanueva, Karen; Timperio, Anna
Title: Built environment and physical activity among adolescents: the moderating effects of neighborhood safety and social support
Publication Title: INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF BEHAVIORAL NUTRITION AND PHYSICAL ACTIVITY
Finding: Objective walkability was associated with increased MVPA outside school hours

Author: Lv, Hewu; Wang, Rui
Title: Association between the built environment and moderate to vigorous leisure-time physical activity among suzhou adolescents: a cross-sectional study
Other PA: walking PA (self report); moderate leisure-time PA (self report); vigorous liesure-time PA (self report)
Finding: Walkability as measured by the NEWS-CC was significantly associated with leisure-time PA

Author: Maddison, R.; Hoorn, SV.; Jiang, Y.; Mhurchu, CN.; Exeter, D.; Dorey, E.; Bullen, C.; Utter, J.; Schaaf, D.; Turley, M.
Title: The environment and physical activity: The influence of psychosocial, perceived and built environmental factors
Publication Title: The international journal of behavioral nutrition and physical activity
Finding: No association between walkability or accessibility and PA

Author: Grow, Helene Mollie; Saelens, Brian E.; Kerr, Jacqueline; Durant, Nefertiti H.; Norman, Gregory J.; Sallis, James F.
Title: Where are youth active? Roles of proximity, active transport, and built environment
Other PA: Activity at location (self report or parent report)
Other transport: Active transport to site (adolescent self report); Active transport to site (child parent report)
Finding: Traffic safety, pedestrian infrastructure, and crime were associated with active transport
Reason: DV is activity at recreation site

Author: McCrorie, Paul; Mitchell, Rich; Macdonald, Laura; Jones, Andrew; Coombes, Emma; Schipperijn, Jasper; Ellaway, Anne
Title: The relationship between living in urban and rural areas of Scotland and children's physical activity and sedentary levels: a country-wide cross-sectional analysis
Finding: Walkability significantly related to household income (less walkable as household income increased). Walkability did not predict any PA outcome.
Reason: No relationship between walkability and PA

Author: McGrath, Leslie J.; Hinckson, Erica A.; Hopkins, Will G.; Mavoa, Suzanne; Witten, Karen; Schofield, Grant
Title: Associations between the neighborhood environment and moderate-to-vigorous walking in new zealand children: Findings from the URBAN study
Study: URBAN (Understanding the Relationship between Activity and Neighborhoods)
Measure(s) of Walkability: Stratified walkability by Frank including retail; SPACES audit
Finding: Upper titile walkability associated with fewer afterschool steps than lower tertile, while weekend and holiday steps higher for middle tertile than lower tertile. MVPA on school commute lower for middle tertile than lower tertive. After school MVPA siginficantly higher for lower tertile walkability than upper tertile. For the SPACES audit, children in neighborhoods in the upper tertile of pedestrian safety ad less MVPA overall, and lower step counts after school and on non-school days. Roads in the upper tertile of attractiveness had highest step count during non-school hours.

Author: Merchant, Anwar T.; Dehghan, Mahshid; Behnke-Cook, Deanna; Anand, Sonia S.
Title: Diet, physical activity, and adiposity in children in poor and rich neighbourhoods: a cross-sectional comparison
Reason: No relationship between walkability and PA

Author: Millstein, Rachel A.; Strobel, Joe; Kerr, Jacqueline; Sallis, James F.; Norman, Gregory J.; Durant, Nefertiti; Harris, Sion; Saelens, Brian E.
Title: Home, school, and neighborhood environment factors and youth physical activity
Other PA: Total PA (self report); Total PA (parent report)
Finding: For younger children, street connectivity was significantly and positively associated with Pa.

Author: Wang, Xiaoge; Liu, Ye; Zhu, Chunwu; Yao, Yao; Helbich, Marco
Title: Associations between the streetscape built environment and walking to school among primary schoolchildren in Beijing, China
Study: Fourth Beijing Official Household Travel Survey
Finding: Walkability lowered odds of ATS. Street safety facility also were associated with lower odds of walking to school.

Author: Molina-García, Javier; Queralt, Ana; Adams, Marc A.; Conway, Terry L.; Sallis, James F.
Title: Neighborhood built environment and socio-economic status in relation to multiple health outcomes in adolescents
Finding: High-SES, high-walkability neighborhoods had the greatest MVPA. Active commuting was more frequent in lower-walkable, higher-SES neighborhoods.

Author: Nakabazzi, B.; Wachira, LM.; Oyeyemi, AL.; Ssenyonga, R.; Onywera, VO.
Title: Parental-perceived home and neighborhood environmental correlates of accelerometer-measured physical activity among school-going children in Uganda
Measure(s) of Walkability: Adapted from Neighborhood Impact on Kids and NEWS-Africa (parent report)
Finding: Higher residential density and lower crime safety were related to children being more likely to meet MVPA guidelines. Generally, few and inconsistent associations of home and neighborhood environments on children's MVPA

Author: Hermosillo-Gallardo, Maria E.; Sebire, Simon J.; Jago, Russell
Title: Perception of safety and its association with physical activity in adolescents in mexico
Other PA: sports (self report), leisure-time PA (self report), PE class (self report)
Finding: Low perception of pedestrian safety was associated with lowere MVPA and sports activity for female adolescents. No association for male adolescents.

Author: Dias, Arieli Fernandes; Fochesatto, Camila Felin; Crochemore-Silva, Inacio; Brand, Caroline; Mota, Jorge; Gaya, Anelise Reis; Gaya, Adroaldo Cezar Araujo
Title: Team sports, running, walking: activity-specific associations with perceived environmental factors in adolescents
Other PA: running (self report), general PA (self report); sports (self report)
Finding: Walkability was not associated with walking behavior or running behavior. Safe street crossing was associated with sports participation, but no other walkability measures were. Walkability was not associated with general PA

Author: Nathan, Andrea; Schipperijn, Jasper; Robinson, Trina; George, Phoebe; Boruff, Bryan; Trost, Stewart G.; Christian, Hayley
Title: The moderating role of parent perceptions in relationships between objectively measured neighbourhood environment attributes and pre-schooler's physical activity: Findings from the PLAYCE study
Finding: Neighborhood parks only associated with energetic play when parents had above average perception of accessibility. Objectively measured street connectivity was associated with less energetic play when pedestrian and traffic safety was perceived to be below average.

Author: Norman, Gregory J.; Nutter, Sandra K.; Ryan, Sherry; Sallis, James F.; Calfas, Karen J.; Patrick, Kevin
Title: Community Design and Access to Recreational Facilities as Correlates of Adolescent Physical Activity and Body-Mass Index
Finding: Girls, MVPA significantly positive with recreation facilities and number of parks. Girls, MVPA significantly negative with intersection density. Boys, MVPA significantly positive with retail floor area.

Author: Hino, Kimihiro; Ikeda, Erika; Sadahiro, Saiko; Inoue, Shigeru
Title: Associations of neighborhood built, safety, and social environment with walking to and from school among elementary school-aged children in Chiba, Japan
Publication Title: INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF BEHAVIORAL NUTRITION AND PHYSICAL ACTIVITY
Finding: For walkability scale, only crime safety was associated with increased odds of walking to school. Traffic safety was associated with walking home from school.

Author: Perez, L. G.; Conway, T. L.; Arredondo, E. M.; Elder, J. P.; Kerr, J.; McKenzie, T. L.; Sallis, J. F.
Title: Where and when adolescents are physically active: Neighborhood environment and psychosocial correlates and their interactions
Other PA: neighborhood lesiure-time PA (self report); non-neighborhood leisure-time PA (self report)
Finding: GIS-based walkability index was not associated with any PA outcome. Parental-perceived walkability was not associated with any PA outcome. Only the interaction between walkability and decisional balance was associated with increased non-school MVPA in girls, and non-neighborhood LTPA in boys.

Author: Yu, Chia-Yuan; Zhu, Xuemei
Title: Impacts of residential self-selection and built environments on children's walking-to-school behaviors
Finding: Self-selection of walkable neighborhood was significant, but not parent's perceived walkability

Author: Pitts, Stephanie B. Jilcott; Carr, Lucas J.; Brinkley, Jason; Byrd, James Langford, III; Crawford, Thomas; Moore, Justin B.
Title: Associations between neighborhood amenity density and health indicators among rural and urban youth
Finding: Walk Score was negatively related to MVPA

Author: Nelson, Melissa C.; Gordon-Larsen, Penny; Song, Yan; Popkin, Barry M.
Title: Built and social environments - Associations with adolescent overweight and activity
Study: National Longitudinal Study of Adolescent Health
Measure(s) of Walkability: Cluster analysis using intersection density, gamma index, cyclomatic index, alpha index
Finding: Adolescents in older suburban developments were more likely to engage in 5 or more bouts of MVPA per week than adolescents in new suburban development. No significant relationship for urban or rural.
Reason: Cluster analysis. Not a direct association.

Author: Caetano, Isabella Toledo; Neves Miranda, Valter Paulo; dos Santos, Fernanda Karina; dos Santos Amorim, Paulo Roberto
Title: Adolescent's movement behaviors and built environment: a latent class analysis
Finding: Adolescents in best perceived environment were less liekly to have adequate LPA time than adolescents in worst perceived environment.

Author: Kerr, J; Rosenberg, D; Sallis, JF; Saelens, BE; Frank, LD; Conway, TL
Title: Active commuting to school: Associations with environment and parental concerns
Publication Title: MEDICINE AND SCIENCE IN SPORTS AND EXERCISE
Measure(s) of Walkability: Stratified based on Frank including retail; NEWS (parent)
Finding: High neighborhood walkability was significantly related with increased odds of an active commute to school. Many items on NEWS were also related. The interaction between walkability and income and walkability and parental concern was also significant.

Author: D'Haese, Sara; De Meester, Femke; De Bourdeaudhuij, Ilse; Deforche, Benedicte; Cardon, Greet
Title: Criterion distances and environmental correlates of active commuting to school in children
Publication Title: INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF BEHAVIORAL NUTRITION AND PHYSICAL ACTIVITY
Finding: Only perceived accessibility by the parents was positively associated with active commuting to school.

Author: Rosenberg, Dori; Ding, Ding; Sallis, James F.; Kerr, Jacqueline; Norman, Gregory J.; Durant, Nefertiti; Harris, Sion K.; Saelens, Brian E.
Title: Neighborhood Environment Walkability Scale for Youth (NEWS-Y): reliability and relationship with physical activity
Other PA: Total PA (self report); Total PA (parent report)
Finding: Low agreement between parent and adolescent reports on the NEWS-Y. Generally positive relationship between NEWS-Y and activity in neighborhood.

Author: Molina-García, Javier; Queralt, Ana
Title: Neighborhood Built Environment and Socioeconomic Status in Relation to Active Commuting to School in Children
Measure(s) of Walkability: Stratified based on Frank excluding retail
Finding: Walkability in the home neighborhood was associated with ATS, but not in the school neighborhood.

Author: Ross, Sharon E. Taverno; Clennin, Morgan N.; Dowda, Marsha; Colabianchi, Natalie; Pate, Russell R.
Title: Stepping it up: Walking behaviors in children transitioning from 5th to 7th grade
Publication Title: INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF ENVIRONMENTAL RESEARCH AND PUBLIC HEALTH
Other transport: Walking for exercise (self report); Walking for transport (self report)
Finding: Girls with a higher Walk Score had higher odds of walking for transportation than girls with a lower Walk Score. No significant relationship was found between walking behavior and Walk Score for boys or for the combined group.

Author: Barnett, A.; Akram, M.; Sit, CH.; Mellecker, R.; Carver, A.; Cerin, E.
Title: Predictors of healthier and more sustainable school travel mode profiles among Hong Kong adolescents
Publication Title: The international journal of behavioral nutrition and physical activity
Finding: No clear relationship reported between walkability and ATS
Other Notes: Hong Kong is most walkable city in Asia. Most students walked sometimes.

Author: Barnett, A.; Sit, CHP.; Mellecker, RR.; Cerin, E.
Title: Associations of socio-demographic, perceived environmental, social and psychological factors with active travel in Hong Kong adolescents: the iHealt(H) cross-sectional study
Finding: Accessibility of places was significantly associated with AT to destinations other than school

Author: Borner, KB.; Mitchell, TB.; Carlson, JA.; Kerr, J.; Saelens, BE.; Schipperijn, J.; Frank, LD.; Conway, TL.; Glanz, K.; Chapman, JE.; Cain, KL.; Sallis, JF.
Title: Latent profile analysis of young adolescents' physical activity across locations on schooldays
Finding: No significant differences were found in walkability between the different profiles in analysis
Other Notes: ATS was used as an independent variable

Author: Bosch, Lander S. M. M.; Wells, Jonathan C. K.; Lum, Sooky; Reid, Alice M.
Title: Associations of the objective built environment along the route to school with children's modes of commuting: A multilevel modelling analysis (the SLIC study)
Study: Size and Lung function In Children study
Finding: The lowest quintile of walkability crossed was significantly related to higher odds of ATS, but not for most walkable vs least walkable.

Author: Bringolf-Isler, B.; Schindler, C.; Kayser, B.; Suggs, LS.; Probst-Hensch, SOPHYA, N;
Title: Objectively measured physical activity in population-representative parent-child pairs: parental modelling matters and is context-specific
Finding: I am confused and need to look into this further

Author: Sallis, James F.; Cain, Kelli L.; Conway, Terry L.; Gavand, Kavita A.; Millstein, Rachel A.; Geremia, Carrie M.; Frank, Lawrence D.; Saelens, Brian E.; Glanz, Karen; King, Abby C.
Title: Is your neighborhood designed to support physical activity? A brief streetscape audit tool
Study: Neighborhood impact on kids; teen environment and neighborhood
Finding: Significant associations between items on MAPS-mini and active transportation. MAPS-Mini only related to leisure PA for children. Commercial areas and transit stops were negatively associated with leisure PA in children.

Author: D'Haese, S.; Gheysen, F.; De, I., Bourdeaudhuij; Deforche, B.; Van, D., Dyck; Cardon, G.
Title: The moderating effect of psychosocial factors in the relation between neighborhood walkability and children's physical activity
Publication Title: The international journal of behavioral nutrition and physical activity
Finding: Walkability had significant negative interactions with friend support and self-efficacy on weekend MVPA. Attitude x walkability had a significant negative association with weekday MVPA.
Reason: No relationship between walkability and PA

Author: Sallis, JF.; Conway, TL.; Cain, KL.; Carlson, JA.; Frank, LD.; Kerr, J.; Glanz, K.; Chapman, JE.; Saelens, BE.
Title: Neighborhood built environment and socioeconomic status in relation to physical activity, sedentary behavior, and weight status of adolescents
Other PA: Total PA outside of school, self-report
Finding: Higher walkability was significantly related to higher MVPA

Author: Stevens, Robert B.; Brown, Barbara B.
Title: Walkable new urban LEED_Neighborhood-Development (LEED-ND) community design and children's physical activity: selection, environmental, or catalyst effects?
Publication Title: INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF BEHAVIORAL NUTRITION AND PHYSICAL ACTIVITY
Finding: Walkability was related to more MVPA during the half hour before and after school. For boys only, walkability related to more MVPA after school.
Other Notes: One of my favorite papers. Very sad to not include it

Author: Dunton, GF.; Intille, SS.; Wolch, J.; Pentz, MA.
Title: Children's perceptions of physical activity environments captured through ecological momentary assessment: a validation study
Finding: No relationship between MVPA and perceived environment characteristics in full model

Author: Ganzar, LA.; Burford, K.; Salvo, D.; Spoon, C.; Sallis, JF.; Hoelscher, DM.
Title: Development, scoring, and reliability for the microscale audit of pedestrian streetscapes for safe routes to school (MAPS-SRTS) instrument

Author: Tappe, Karyn A.; Glanz, Karen; Sallis, James F.; Zhou, Chuan; Saelens, Brian E.
Title: Children’s physical activity and parents’ perception of the neighborhood environment: neighborhood impact on kids study
Publication Title: International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity
Finding: No significant relationship found between walkability and objectively measured MVPA

Author: Holt, N. L.; Spence, J. C.; Sehn, Z. L.; Cutumisu, N.
Title: Neighborhood and developmental differences in children's perceptions of opportunities for play and physical activity
Finding: Children in high walkable neighborhood depicted more active transport than children in low walkable neighborhood

Author: Huang, SJ.; Hung, WC.; Sharpe, PA.; Wai, JP.
Title: Neighborhood environment and physical activity among urban and rural schoolchildren in Taiwan
Finding: Walkability was not significantly different between urban and rural environments, but accessibility was much higher for urban environments. Walkability was not significantly associated with PA, but accessibility had a positive association with PA.
Reason: Cluster analysis. Not a direct association.

Author: Huertas-Delgado, Francisco Javier; Mertens, Lieze; Chillon, Palma; Van Dyck, Delfien
Title: Parents' and adolescents' perception of traffic- and crime-related safety as correlates of independent mobility among Belgian adolescents

Author: Terron-Perez, Marta; Molina-Garcia, Javier; Santaines-Borreda, Elena; Estevan, Isaac; Queralt, Ana
Title: Using the MAPS-global audit tool to assess the influence of microscale built-environment attributes related to physical activity and sedentary behavior in spanish youth
Other transport: Active communiting in neighborhood, excluding trips to school (self report)
Finding: One MAPS global scale had a significant positive relationship with MVPA, but not the overall score. The overall scores had significant positive relationships to active commuting.

Author: Jauregui, A.; Soltero, E.; Santos-Luna, R.; Hern√°ndez-Barrera, L.; Barquera, S.; J√°uregui, E.; L√©vesque, L.; L√≥pez-Taylor, J.; Ortiz-Hern√°ndez, L.; Lee, R.
Title: A multisite study of environmental correlates of active commuting to school in mexican children

Author: Kurka, JM.; Adams, MA.; Todd, M.; Colburn, T.; Sallis, JF.; Cain, KL.; Glanz, K.; Frank, LD.; Saelens, BE.
Title: Patterns of neighborhood environment attributes in relation to children's physical activity
Finding: Out-of-school MVPA was significantly lower for low walkable unsafe neighborhoods

Author: Larouche, R.; B√©langer, M.; Brussoni, M.; Faulkner, G.; Gunnell, K.; Tremblay, MS.
Title: Correlates of active school transportation during the COVID-19 pandemic among canadian 7- to 12-year-olds: a national study

Author: Tung, S. E. H.; Ng, X. H.; Chin, Y. S.; Taib, M. N. Mohd
Title: Associations between parents' perception of neighbourhood environments and safety with physical activity of primary school children in Klang, Selangor, Malaysia
Finding: Parent's perception of accessible land use mix and traffic safety was associated with PA for the child.

Author: Liao, Bojing; E. W. van den Berg, Pauline; van Wesemael, Pieter J. V.; A. Arentze, Theo
Title: How Does Walkability Change Behavior? A Comparison between Different Age Groups in the Netherlands
Publication Title: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health

Author: Voorhees, Carolyn C.; Yan, Alice F.; Clifton, Kelly J.; Wang, Min Qi
Title: Neighborhood environment, self-efficacy, and physical activity in urban adolescents
Finding: Land use mix and neighborhood satisfaction were associated with walking. Land use mix was associated with MVPA.

Author: Mah, SK.; Nettlefold, L.; Macdonald, HM.; Winters, M.; Race, D.; Voss, C.; McKay, HA.
Title: Does parental support influence children's active school travel?

Author: Wayas, Feyisayo A.; Smith, Joanne A.; Lambert, Estelle V.; Guthrie-Dixon, Natalie; Wasnyo, Yves; West, Sacha; Oni, Tolu; Foley, Louise
Title: Association of Perceived Neighbourhood Walkability with Self-Reported Physical Activity and Body Mass Index in South African Adolescents
Publication Title: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
Finding: Inconsisttent findings

Author: Mitáš, J.; Sas-Nowosielski, K.; Groffik, D.; Frömel, K.
Title: The safety of the neighborhood environment and physical activity in czech and polish adolescents
Publication Title: International journal of environmental research and public health
Finding: Significant association between environmental safety and total PA and lesiure-time vigorous PA. Total PA was negatively associated with environmental safety. Vigorous PA. Leisure-time total PA was higher for girls living in higher environmental safety neighborhoods.

Author: Panter, JR.; Jones, AP.; van, EM., Sluijs; Griffin, SJ.
Title: Attitudes, social support and environmental perceptions as predictors of active commuting behaviour in school children
Publication Title: Journal of epidemiology and community health

Author: Roberts, JD.; Knight, B.; Ray, R.; Saelens, BE.
Title: Parental perceived built environment measures and active play in Washington DC metropolitan children
Finding: Many significant associations between perceived BE and children. Not really a clear finding on walkability though

Author: Xu, Huilan; Wen, Li Ming; Hardy, Louise L.; Rissel, Chris
Title: Mothers' perceived neighbourhood environment and outdoor play of 2-to 3.5-year-old children: Findings from the healthy beginnings trial
Publication Title: INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF ENVIRONMENTAL RESEARCH AND PUBLIC HEALTH
Finding: Walk Score was not associated with odds of outdoor playtime, but perceived safe outdoor play was

Author: Salvy, SJ.; Feda, DM.; Epstein, LH.; Roemmich, JN.
Title: The social context moderates the relationship between neighborhood safety and adolescents' activities
Finding: Neighborhood safety was negatively associated with PA alone, but positively associated with PA when interacted by time with peers

Author: Silva, AAPD.; Fermino, RC.; Souza, CA.; Lima, AV.; Rodriguez-A√±ez, CR.; Reis, RS.
Title: Socioeconomic status moderates the association between perceived environment and active commuting to school

Author: Van, D., Dyck; De, I., Bourdeaudhuij; Cardon, G.; Deforche, B.
Title: Criterion distances and correlates of active transportation to school in Belgian older adolescents
Publication Title: The international journal of behavioral nutrition and physical activity
Reason: No relationship between walkability and PA
